elements.sent-2411-001: Resource elasticity is one of the key defining characteristics of the Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) serverless computing paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-002: In order to provide strong multi-tenant isolation, FaaS providers commonly sandbox functions inside virtual machines (VMs or microVMs).
elements.sent-2411-003: While compute resources assigned to VM-sandboxed functions can be seamlessly adjusted on the fly, memory elasticity remains challenging, especially when scaling down.
elements.sent-2411-004: State-of-the-art mechanisms for VM memory elasticity suffer from increased reclaim latency when memory needs to be released, compounded by CPU and memory bandwidth overheads.
elements.sent-2411-005: We identify the obliviousness of the Linux memory manager to the virtually hotplugged memory as the key issue hindering hot-unplug performance, and design HotMem, a novel approach for fast and efficient VM memory hot(un)plug, targeting VM-sandboxed serverless functions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our key insight is that by segregating virtually hotplugged memory regions from regular VM memory, we are able to bound the lifetimes of allocations within these regions thus enabling their fast and efficient reclamation.
elements.sent-2411-007: We implement HotMem in Linux v6.6 and our evaluation shows that it is an order of magnitude faster than state-of-practice to reclaim VM memory, while achieving the same P99 function latency with a model that statically over-provisions VMs.
elements.sent-2411-008: Serverless computing, in the form of the Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) execution model, has in recent years solidified its status as the dominant cloud computing paradigm studied by both industry and academia~\cite{awslambda,azure,googleserverless,alibaba,huawei,cloudflare .}
elements.sent-2411-009: FaaS applications are developed as standalone functions, designed to be highly available.
elements.sent-2411-010: Users typically provide their source code and providers are responsible to secure the dependencies (e.g., runtime) and the resources (e.g., memory, CPU) for the functions to run.
elements.sent-2411-011: One of the defining features of FaaS is its pay-as-you-go model, in which providers execute functions on-demand and charge users only for the resources consumed during function execution.
elements.sent-2411-012: This critical feature requires up- and down-scaling compute and memory resources dynamically and transparently following the incoming load.
elements.sent-2411-013: Typically, providers scale the resources by increasing or decreasing the number of function instances that serve requests.
elements.sent-2411-014: A new function instance is created when there are no other available instances to handle the incoming request(s) within the typical Service Level Objective (SLO) agreement (e.g., tail latency).
elements.sent-2411-015: A function instance is evicted when the incoming load falls below a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, in order to reach the required SLOs with high resource efficiency, the increase and decrease of the number of functions according to the incoming load needs to be paired with an appropriate provisioning and de-provisioning of actual resources.
elements.sent-2411-017: Functions are commonly sandboxed within either containers, e.g., Docker~\cite{docker , or (micro) virtual machines (microVMs), e.g., Firecracker~\cite{firecracker .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: FaaS providers typically eschew containerization for the strong isolation guarantees of microVMs.
elements.sent-2411-019: When deploying functions in microVMs, FaaS providers opt for different isolation models, namely: a) the single-container-per-VM model, i.e. each function instance is deployed in a dedicated microVM (AWS Lambda~\cite{awslambda ), or b) the multi-container-per-VM model, i.e. multiple instances share the same microVM (Azure~\cite{azurefunctions ).}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The above strategies offer different trade-offs in terms of performance and resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-021: Serving an incoming request from a freshly-spawned function instance (cold start) severely impacts tail latency compared to using a cached (warm) function instance~\cite{peeking,azuretrace .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Cold-start delays, though, vary significantly based on the isolation policy.
elements.sent-2411-023: Functions on dedicated microVMs suffer from higher start-up delays as cold runs include booting the Guest OS and initializing the function's runtime.
elements.sent-2411-024: Instead, sharing the VM decouples new instances from such initialization costs and reduces the memory tax per instance as they typically share some of their runtime dependencies (e.g., libraries).
elements.sent-2411-025: However, this method requires overprovisioned VMs (e.g., in terms of CPU and / or memory resources) to be able to scale up the number of instances within them when the incoming load increases.
elements.sent-2411-026: This has a significant impact on resource utilization, as the VMs tie down idle resources, especially when the incoming load is low.
elements.sent-2411-027: We identify this rigid resource allocation as a significant disadvantage of the multi-container-per-VM model and explore a solution to dynamically and efficiently resize VM resources.
elements.sent-2411-028: While compute resources can be seamlessly adjusted on the fly~\cite{harvestserverless,hvm , memory elasticity for VMs remains challenging, especially when scaling memory down.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The state-of-practice interfaces, namely memory ballooning~\cite{balloonvmware,resizingballoon and vDIMM memory hotplugging~\cite{hotplugkernel , suffer from unreliable or unpredictably slow memory reclamation, impact guest performance, and can induce fragmentation on both host and guest~\cite{virtio-mem,hotplugorballon,freepages .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, virtio-mem is not designed to provide the rapid memory elasticity required by the FaaS execution model, since, as we show, it requires multiple seconds to resize a VM after 10s of function instances are recycled (scaled down).
elements.sent-2411-031: The bursty access pattern of serverless functions, however, frequently requires up and down scaling resources in sub-second intervals~\cite{huawei-cold .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Providing an optimized hotplugging interface is only part of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-033: Processes that run in a VM (e.g., function instances) tend to have interleaved footprints due to the lazy memory allocation employed by modern OS.
elements.sent-2411-034: When a process exits, the released (idle) memory is fragmented, requiring the migration of interleaved occupied pages to be reclaimed.
elements.sent-2411-035: These migrations dominate the performance of VM down-sizing~\cite{hotplugorballon .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We argue that memory hotplugging interfaces need to be coupled with informed memory management in the guest to ensure fast and reliable memory reclamation.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work we introduce HotMem, a novel mechanism that extends the memory manager and the hot(un)plug interface of the OS in order to achieve rapid VM memory reclamation for serverless functions.
elements.sent-2411-038: HotMem follows a special purpose design, tailored to resizing VMs running function instances under the multi-container-per-VM serverless model.
elements.sent-2411-039: It leverages the predictable memory requirements of the FaaS execution model, i.e., the fact that the memory limits for each function instance are defined by the user, to segregate the memory allocations of functions that run concurrently inside a VM.
elements.sent-2411-040: This eliminates the interleaving of their footprints and hence enables the fast reclamation of their memory resources when they exit.
elements.sent-2411-041: To realize this, HotMem partitions guest memory to chunks able to contain the entire footprint of a single function instance and extends the OS memory manager to assign every instance to such a dedicated partition.
elements.sent-2411-042: It then serves all the allocation requests of the instance from this dedicated partition.
elements.sent-2411-043: It also extends the hot(un)plug interface to be aware of these partitions, i.e., to dynamically populate them during plug operations and to shrink them instantly during unplug requests.
elements.sent-2411-044: Unplugging is nearly optimal as it involves only the removal of empty partitions without migrating any occupied pages.
elements.sent-2411-045: By avoiding migrations, HotMem also eliminates the memory bandwidth and CPU interference of hotplugging, which can impact the performance of other concurrently running functions during VM down-sizing~\cite{mhvm .}
elements.sent-2411-046: We show how HotMem can be integrated with an Open-whisk-based FaaS runtime~\cite{faasmem,openwhisk to dynamically up- and down-scale VM resources to closely match the up- and down-scaling of function instances that run within each VM.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Our evaluation reveals that HotMem is an order-of-magnitude faster than state-of-practice in reclaiming VM memory, typically allowing sub-second reclamation of multiple GiBs memory.
elements.sent-2411-048: It also achieves comparable P99 latency with statically over-provisioned VMs, while dynamically up- and down-scaling resources under realistic load~\cite{azuretrace .}
elements.sent-2411-049: In order to provide inter-tenant isolation, FaaS providers sandbox functions running in public clouds in isolated environments.
elements.sent-2411-050: A common practice is to ship and run function code and dependencies as a container~\cite{docker .}
elements.sent-2411-051: However, containers based on OS-level virtualization do not provide strong security and isolation guarantees~\cite{lightvm .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Consequently, FaaS providers typically resort to hardware level isolation, provided by virtual machines (VMs)~\cite{firecracker , for function sandboxing.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Containers encapsulating function code and dependencies are then run inside a VM.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{./figures/Motivation/IsolationModels-crop} \caption{The 1:1 and N:1 serverless models. N:1 fragments and ties down idle memory resources.} \label{fig:motiv:models}
elements.sent-2411-054: FaaS providers use two main models~\cite{rund to securely deploy function instances into virtual machines: i) the multi-contai-ner-per-VM (N:1)~\cite{azure,azurefunctions and ii) the single-container-per-VM (1:1)~\cite{awslambda model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: In a nutshell, the multi-container-per-VM model scales up the number of function instances (containers) of the same user within the same VM to handle spikes in incoming load.
elements.sent-2411-057: The single-container-per-VM model deploys instead each function instance in a lightweight VM, i.e., a microVM, and scales up the number of VMs during bursts of requests.
elements.sent-2411-058: While both models are adopted by the industry ~\cite{azure,awslambda , each presents its own trade-offs with respect to i) cold start delays, ii) resource sharing, and iii) resource allocation.. Cold starts.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Spawning a function instance to serve incoming requests comes with significant cold start delays~\cite{huawei-trace,huawei-cold,azuretrace,peeking , as a new sandbox (container) must be created and the new instance's runtime dependencies and private state must be initialized.}
elements.sent-2411-060: This is the reason why providers typically cache idle instances for fixed time windows (keep-alive)~\cite{azuretrace,faascache or delay spawning new instances as much as possible~\cite{openwhisk-scheduler , in an attempt to minimize cold start invocations.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The 1:1 model adds the extra non-negligible overhead of booting a new microVM to spawn a new instance.
elements.sent-2411-062: While a long line of research attempts to optimize away boot costs~\cite{vhive, faasnap, peeking, fireworks,proghorn , starting a VM is in principle a more expensive operation than creating a new container~\cite{survey,agile .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Moreover, the N:1 model can potentially avoid some of the runtime initialization overheads, as the dependencies of the function are typically already warm in the shared VM, i.e., already cached by the guest OS.
elements.sent-2411-064: Another trade-off is related to memory footprints.
elements.sent-2411-065: The 1:1 model introduces higher memory overheads as it requires a separate guest OS kernel and function runtime per function instance.
elements.sent-2411-066: On the other hand, the N:1 model shares the OS and runtime dependencies among the instances that are co-located within the same VM.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, the function's language libraries are cached by the guest OS and reused across instances.
elements.sent-2411-068: One of the key aspects of serverless computing is that the user demands a specific maximum amount of CPU and memory resources for each function to execute~\cite{huawei-trace .}
elements.sent-2411-069: This allows the provider to optimize the resource allocation on the server side, based on these fine grained, predictable, and predefined requirements.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the single-container-single-VM model (1:1) the resources allocated per microVM closely follow the target function's resource limits.
elements.sent-2411-071: The allocation is simplified, the function size along with the sandbox overhead (e.g., microVM and container memory requirements) are calculated as a single unit that needs to optimally fit in a set of available server nodes.
elements.sent-2411-072: More importantly when the provider decides to scale down function resources, it can shut down a microVM and instantly release a predictable amount of memory.
elements.sent-2411-073: On the other hand, on the multi-container-single VM model (N:1), the resource allocation is more complicated and subject to the concurrency factor, i.e., the number of function instances provisioned to be deployed within the same VM.
elements.sent-2411-074: For this model, VMs are typically created with enough memory to accommodate multiple instances.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, as the number of required instances varies over time, e.g., based on the incoming load, this model frequently results in memory fragmentation~\cite{rund,survey .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Figure~\ref{fig:motiv:models shows how allocated VM memory is idling when the load is low and the number of instances has been scaled down by the serverless runtime.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The VM still ties down its maximum resources in the host.
elements.sent-2411-078: As the VMs are now larger, they exacerbate memory stranding~\cite{pond,mhvm .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Figure~\ref{fig:motiv:models shows how overprovisioned VMs can lead to wasted server memory.}
elements.sent-2411-080: A natural way to address the problem is to reclaim fragmented memory and CPU resources at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-081: While CPU reclamation is well studied ~\cite{rund,harvestserverless,azurespot,awsspot,hvm , in the next section we discuss VM memory elasticity and elaborate on the challenges of resizing VM memory resources, especially within the tight latency limits of serverless~\cite{rund .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In this section we discuss the overheads involved in re-sizing a VM's memory using vDIMM-based memory hotplugging~\cite{virtio-mem,hotplugkernel .}
elements.sent-2411-083: We focus on vDIMM hotplugging as it is the mechanism employed by virtio-mem~\cite{virtio-mem , the state-of-the-art for dynamically adjusting VM memory.}
elements.sent-2411-084: We discuss other interfaces, such as ballooning~\cite{xen,vmware-balloon , in Section~\ref{sec:related .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Memory hotplugging dynamically adjusts VM memory by emulating in the hypervisor the hotplugging of physical memory DIMMs.
elements.sent-2411-086: The VM is notified, e.g., via ACPI for x86 VMs, about the hotplug operation and then relies on the OS to add or remove the DIMM memory to the system.
elements.sent-2411-087: We elaborate on the mechanisms used by Linux to add (online) or remove (offline) DIMMs in the next paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-088: The default DIMM hotplug interface is tailored for (un)plugging physical memory, and thus has various inherent limitations.
elements.sent-2411-089: It only operates in large (DIMM-sized) granularities and requires architecture-specific support.
elements.sent-2411-090: It exposes a new device, i.e., a parativirtualized DIMM, which it chunks up in smaller slots.
elements.sent-2411-091: Each of these slots can be plugged and unplugged independently, thus allowing the adjustment of VM memory in smaller quanta.
elements.sent-2411-092: Although virtio-mem improves upon the default DIMM hotplug, multiple works~\cite{mhvm,hotplugorballon,hubballon show that DIMM memory hotplugging, with or without virtio-mem, suffers from overheads stemming from page migrations.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Linux manages physical memory in the granularity of pages (i.e., 4KiB) but adds and removes ((un)plugs) memory in the granularity of memory blocks, i.e., 128MiB for x86.
elements.sent-2411-094: Hot-add updates the OS metadata for physical memory, such as the Linux memory map array (memmap), and is penalized by the overheads of creating and initializing the struct pages that represent each 4KiB range of the block.
elements.sent-2411-095: Online releases these pages to the OS allocator, to expose the plugged memory to the system as usable.
elements.sent-2411-096: The first step dominates unplug performance as it has to isolate the pages of the block to be unplugged and this commonly involves migrating occupied pages.
elements.sent-2411-097: The OS needs to zero out memory for security and correctness reasons.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, multiple user-space libraries (e.g., libc) assume that newly allocated memory is zeroed-out.
elements.sent-2411-099: Linux has two modes to guarantee this: a) it either zeros out memory pages during allocation (init\_on\_alloc), or b) release (init\_on\_free).
elements.sent-2411-100: This is a boot-time system-wide configuration with implications on (un)plug performance.
elements.sent-2411-101: Init\_on\_alloc penalizes unplug operations, as unplugging uses generic allocation routines to reserve the memory to be offlined from the rest of the system.
elements.sent-2411-102: Init\_on\_free penalizes plug operations as pages need to be zeroed out before they can be released to the OS allocator for onlining.
elements.sent-2411-103: Firstly, the OS memory allocator can place non-movable data~\cite{contiguitas,making , e.g., kernel memory, on multiple memory blocks, which would prevent them from offlining.}
elements.sent-2411-104: To address these issues, Linux has introduced a special memory zone (ZONE\_MOVABLE), to distinguish movable and non-movable pages.
elements.sent-2411-105: ZONE\_MOVABLE serves all allocation requests that correspond to data that in principle can be migrated, e.g., user-space allocations and file system caching layers (i.e., page cache).
elements.sent-2411-106: The memory blocks that populate this zone can always be successfully offlined, as long as there is enough free memory in the rest of the system to migrate their occupied pages.
elements.sent-2411-107: Linux uses other zones, e.g., ZONE\_DMA and ZONE\_NORMAL, to serve allocations for unmovable data and to fall back for movable data when ZONE\_MOVABLE is full.
elements.sent-2411-108: Memory that is hot-plugged is typically added to ZONE\_MOVABLE to guarantee the success of any future unplug operations.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{./figures/zones-crop} \caption{Processes footprints are commonly interleaved within a single physical memory block, thus when F2 exits, F1 and F2 own pages that are co-located in the same blocks and need to be migrated to reclaim memory.} \label{fig:vanilla-zones}
elements.sent-2411-109: While ZONE\_MOVABLE guarantees that offlining will succeed, it does not necessarily speed it up.
elements.sent-2411-110: The OS commonly interleaves the footprint of different processes~\cite{ranger,capaging on multiple blocks of physical memory during memory allocation.}
elements.sent-2411-111: This leads to expensive migrations of interleaved pages when the OS tries to reclaim the memory of one of the processes that exited.
elements.sent-2411-112: Let us consider a common scenario when it comes to virtual machine resizing.
elements.sent-2411-113: Figure~\ref{fig:vanilla-zones depicts a VM with 3 running processes.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The OS has mapped their virtual address spaces only with pages from ZONE\_MOVABLE, but their footprints are interleaved across multiple physical memory blocks inside the zone.
elements.sent-2411-115: Linux allocates memory lazily, at the page granularity (4KiB or 2MiB), when the processes access their pages for the first time (page fault).
elements.sent-2411-116: To serve the faults, the OS allocates any available page, hindering contiguous mappings and scattering the workload's footprint across blocks.
elements.sent-2411-117: This is the reason why the depicted applications footprints are interleaved.
elements.sent-2411-118: This interleaving eventually penalizes memory unplugging.
elements.sent-2411-119: For example, in Figure~\ref{fig:vanilla-zones , when the process F2 terminates and releases its memory pages, the serverless runtime would attempt to down-size the VM to reclaim the released memory.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The hypervisor sends a request to the guest OS to unplug resources equal to the size of the terminated process footprint.
elements.sent-2411-121: The guest will try to isolate (offline) memory (e.g., via virtio-mem) that matches the requested size by iterating over the guest's physical memory blocks.
elements.sent-2411-122: Due to the interleaving of process footprints, most memory blocks in the system will be at least partially occupied, thus the guest has to a) migrate some pages to isolate enough memory blocks and b) then offline and unplug them.
elements.sent-2411-123: This can significantly penalize unplug performance.
elements.sent-2411-124: Hot-unplugging memory can penalize the performance of processes running in the VM that is being down-sized.
elements.sent-2411-125: Related work~\cite{virtio-mem has shown that the migration of occupied pages hogs precious CPU resources interfering with other processes.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We observe that one of the reasons why (un)plugging suffers from long latencies is that it attempts to blindly reclaim guest OS memory.
elements.sent-2411-127: The hypervisor is unaware of the guest free memory blocks, due to the virtualization barrier, and thus scans the guest physical memory linearly, migrating occupied pages to isolate and remove memory blocks.
elements.sent-2411-128: While it is very hard to identify and isolate free memory on a general purpose VM, hosting multiple long-running workload(s) with working sets that vary over time and input, we observe that memory usage is far more predictable for the serverless use-case.
elements.sent-2411-129: The occupied and free memory inside a VM can be identified and managed at the granularity of the deployed function's memory resource limits, as defined by the user.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, to be able to add and remove such coarse-grain blocks fast, avoiding migrations or free page searches, special mechanisms are necessary to guarantee their isolation.
elements.sent-2411-131: The goal of this work is to provide such mechanisms to dynamically adjust VM memory for the serverless use-case.
elements.sent-2411-132: We aim to reduce the memory fragmentation that severely penalizes the original N:1 model (Section~\ref{sec:motiv:isolation ).}
elements.sent-2411-133: We build on the existing memory hot(un)plugging mechanisms and attempt to address their performance pathologies, identified in Section~\ref{sec:motiv:resize , for this specific use-case.}
elements.sent-2411-134: To that end, we design HotMem, an OS interface to efficiently manage the memory of serverless functions, enabling its rapid reclamation.
elements.sent-2411-135: Aligned to our analysis in Section~\ref{sec:motiv , we set the following main goals: HotMem should be able to follow the memory requirements of the total number of alive function instances running inside the same VM, scaling up and down its memory resources accordingly.}
elements.sent-2411-136: HotMem should also be fast enough to cope with the bursty access pattern of serverless functions that frequently requires the up- and down-scaling of resources in sub-second intervals~\cite{huawei-cold .}
elements.sent-2411-137: HotMem should allow the sharing of state, e.g., libraries, across the function instances that run in the same VM to preserve the memory saving benefits of the N:1 model.
elements.sent-2411-138: HotMem should not affect the performance of already running function instances inside the VM.
elements.sent-2411-139: To achieve these goals HotMem introduces two mechanisms:.
elements.sent-2411-140: It partitions guest memory across running function instances to isolate their footprints and prevent their interleaving.
elements.sent-2411-141: It makes the OS unplug interface (i.e., virtio-mem) aware of these partitions, to enable the fast and zero-interference reclamation of instances memory blocks as they terminate.
elements.sent-2411-143: HotMem leverages the pre-defined memory requirements of function instances to be deployed in a VM under the N:1 model to partition the available guest memory into fixed-sized chunks.
elements.sent-2411-144: Each chunk can host a single instance.
elements.sent-2411-145: For this, HotMem introduces a new memory zone in the OS memory manager (ZONE\_HotMem) that supports partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-146: HotMem memory cannot be used for generic allocations; it is reserved from the main OS allocation path.
elements.sent-2411-147: Instead, an instance has to explicitly ask to be backed by HotMem resources, using a dedicated API described in Section~\ref{sec:implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-148: In that case, it is mapped to a HotMem partition and all its allocations (i.e., anonymous memory) are served from it.
elements.sent-2411-149: Once a partition is assigned to an instance, it is locked until the instance terminates.
elements.sent-2411-150: Figure~\ref{fig:HotMem-overview shows how different instances are isolated into different memory partitions under HotMem.}
elements.sent-2411-151: A HotMem partition is populated by a plug event, triggered by a serverless runtime (Section~\ref{sec:runtime ).}
elements.sent-2411-153: To preserve the memory savings of the N:1 model, HotMem employs a shared partition per VM to back the file mappings of all instances.
elements.sent-2411-154: File mappings typically correspond to libraries and runtime dependencies shared across instances, i.e., they are instantiated once in memory and mapped multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-155: This partition is pre-populated at boot time for each VM based on the characteristics of each function.
elements.sent-2411-156: The anonymous mappings of the instances are backed by private partitions as discussed above.. Partition-aware unplugging.
elements.sent-2411-157: As instances run isolated into HotMem partitions, unplugging the memory of a terminated instance involves zero-migrations and thus zero-interference under HotMem.
elements.sent-2411-158: Unplug operations are triggered by a serverless runtime (Section~\ref{sec:runtime ) when instance(s) inside a VM are recycled due to a drop in the incoming load.}
elements.sent-2411-159: HotMem extends the unplug interface of the OS to directly identify the HotMem partitions that are emptied when the instance(s) terminate, and instantly offlines and removes the corresponding memory blocks without having to migrate any occupied pages.
elements.sent-2411-160: We implement HotMem in Linux kernel 6.6 extending: i) the memory manager and ii) the virtio-mem hot(un)plug guest driver.. HotMem partitions.
elements.sent-2411-161: HotMem partitions the guest physical memory into chunks that will host non-interleaved function instances.
elements.sent-2411-162: The size of each HotMem partition must match the memory requirements of the function to be deployed in the specific VM under the N:1 serverless model.
elements.sent-2411-163: This parameter is known when the VM is set-up by a serverless runtime, as the memory resource limits of each function are defined by the user (Section~\ref{sec:runtime ).}
elements.sent-2411-164: Thus we configure the partition size as a boot parameter for the guest OS.
elements.sent-2411-165: We implement HotMem partitions as different zones (zone structs) in the kernel, following the original ZONE\_MOVABLE.
elements.sent-2411-166: Each such structure represents a guest physical memory region that populates a HotMem partition (e.g., holding its starting address and size), is stored in the per-NUMA node zonelists of the kernel (node\_zones).
elements.sent-2411-167: Each partition is uniquely identified by its partition id.
elements.sent-2411-168: HotMem currently supports only one memory node per VM.
elements.sent-2411-169: We consider NUMA topologies as future work.
elements.sent-2411-170: At boot time, we create $N$ HotMem zone structures, that initially link to empty partitions, thus setting the maximum concurrent functions that can be supported by each VM, i.e., only $N$ instances can be concurrently deployed in the VM at any point in time.
elements.sent-2411-171: We use the term concurrency factor for $N$ and expose it as a boot parameter to be set by the serverless runtime.
elements.sent-2411-172: Note that unlike an over-provisioned VM, this design pre-sets the maximum concurrency but does not pre-allocate the corresponding memory resources.
elements.sent-2411-173: The $N$ HotMem partitions are initially empty, at boot time; they are not backed by physical memory pages.
elements.sent-2411-174: They are instead populated and emptied dynamically by plug and unplug operations.
elements.sent-2411-175: HotMem populates only one partition at boot time, the shared HotMem partition, that is dedicated to store shared runtime and language dependencies across concurrently running function instances in the VM.
elements.sent-2411-176: Its size is also a boot time parameter set by the serverless runtime.
elements.sent-2411-178: The HotMem partition plugging starts when the hotplug driver inside the guest, i.e virtio-mem, receives a plug request from the hypervisor.
elements.sent-2411-179: The driver becomes aware that a specific range, corresponding to the partition(s) size, of its managed memory has been plugged by the hypervisor and uses the kernel memory onlining interfaces accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-180: We intercept the onlining process in order to instruct the kernel to correlate the memory range with the corresponding HotMem partition(s), populate the free pages of the HotMem partition and notify the users of the interface, as explained in the next paragraph.
elements.sent-2411-181: We note that if the system is configured to zero pages upon release (Section \ref{sec:background:linux ), we also modify the onlining path to skip zeroing HotMem pages upon plugging, as they are already zeroed in the host.. The HotMem interface.}
elements.sent-2411-182: We design a system-call based interface for HotMem, which allows the assignment of populated HotMem partitions to function instances.
elements.sent-2411-183: The interface allows the calling process to request the OS to serve its memory allocations via HotMem partitions.
elements.sent-2411-184: For each such request, we scan the list of HotMem partitions (zonelist) in order to find an available (empty) HotMem partition.
elements.sent-2411-185: Upon success, the partition is marked as reserved, increasing a reference counter, i.e. partition\_users, and its partition id becomes associated with the calling process.
elements.sent-2411-186: Per-partition locks are used to avoid race conditions for concurrent requests.
elements.sent-2411-187: Linux uses a memory descriptor (mm\_struct) to represent each process's address space.
elements.sent-2411-188: We add a new field in the memory descriptor to store the partition id that is assigned to each HotMem process.
elements.sent-2411-189: This is then used on the memory allocation path, in order to only allocate pages from the specific partition for the process.
elements.sent-2411-190: As a HotMem process starts executing inside the VM, its virtual memory accesses trigger the lazy allocation of guest physical memory through page fault traps.
elements.sent-2411-191: HotMem intercepts the faults of HotMem processes and implements a dedicated handler that allocates pages from the HotMem partition that is assigned to the process, instead of allocating pages from generic and shared OS zones (e.g. from ZONE\_MOVABLE).
elements.sent-2411-192: This guarantees the confinement of the footprint of each HotMem process within a single partition.
elements.sent-2411-193: To preserve the sharing of language and runtime dependencies among the instances running in the VM, HotMem distinguishes between faults for anonymous and file mappings.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the first, it allocates pages from the process-assigned HotMem partition, as described above.
elements.sent-2411-195: For the latter, it uses the system-wide shared HotMem partition, as described in previous paragraphs.
elements.sent-2411-196: It faults-in each file page once and subsequently maps it to the address space of any HotMem process that touches it upon a fault.
elements.sent-2411-197: Essentially, this design implements a file caching layer for all function instances via a dedicated shared HotMem partition.
elements.sent-2411-198: If no available partitions exist, then the interface puts the requesting process in a waitqueue.
elements.sent-2411-199: Processes on the waitqueue are woken up upon two events: a) a plug operation that populates a HotMem partition, and b) the release of a previously occupied HotMem partition, i.e. when its partition\_users variable drops to 0.
elements.sent-2411-200: As discussed above, HotMem applies the user-set memory limits of each function via the HotMem partition size.
elements.sent-2411-201: If a function tries to allocate more memory than the size of the HotMem partition, OS mechanisms (e.g, the OOM Killer) are triggered to kill the HotMem process and prevent violations of partition isolation.
elements.sent-2411-202: When a HotMem process exits, its memory descriptor is destroyed.
elements.sent-2411-203: We intercept this path to also decrement the reference counter of its dedicated HotMem partition (partition\_users).
elements.sent-2411-204: When the counter drops to zero the partition, the interface kicks the HotMem waitqueue described above to notify any waiting processes that a HotMem partition is now available.
elements.sent-2411-205: Every time a HotMem process calls the clone system call to create a new thread or process, we assign the child to the parent's HotMem partition, i.e., parent and children share the same partition.
elements.sent-2411-206: The intuition is that serverless functions by default do not call fork to scale~\cite{MXFaas,sock , i.e., they don't call fork to create new function instances.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Thus we co-locate all threads and processes of a function instance on the same HotMem partition.
elements.sent-2411-208: We also increment the partition reference counter (partition\_users) as multiple memory descriptors now link to it.
elements.sent-2411-210: The unplug operation starts upon receiving a request from the hypervisor.
elements.sent-2411-211: The hotplug driver, i.e virtio-mem, inside the guest, keeps track of free HotMem partition(s) via their reference counter.
elements.sent-2411-212: When the driver receives an unplug operation from the hypervisor, it immediately offlines and removes a free partition without migrating any of the pages, as the partition is guaranteed to be empty.
elements.sent-2411-213: The serverless runtime coordinates the hypervisor unplug operations with the function recycle operations.
elements.sent-2411-214: It thus can know the exact number of greed HotMem partitions in the VM.
elements.sent-2411-215: We also optimize the offlining path to exclude page zeroing by intercepting the generic allocation routines called during unplug as explained in Section~\ref{sec:background:linux , as pages will be zeroed by the host.}
elements.sent-2411-216: The virtio-mem driver then informs the hypervisor that the memory blocks corresponding to the unplugged HotMem partition(s) have been removed.
elements.sent-2411-217: The hypervisor immediately releases the pages to the host by using madvise to mark them MADV\_DONTNEED.
elements.sent-2411-218: HotMem is an OS mechanism designed for the rapid reclamation of VM memory resources of serverless function instances when they terminate.
elements.sent-2411-219: A serverless runtime can leverage the HotMem interface in order to (un)plug and (de)allocate HotMem memory as it scales up and down function instances in the N:1 serverless model.
elements.sent-2411-220: Figure~\ref{fig:HotMem-overview shows how HotMem can be integrated in such a runtime.}
elements.sent-2411-221: For simplicity, we show the scaling up / down workflow for only one function instance.
elements.sent-2411-222: However, the same applies when the runtime concurrently scales up or down multiple function instances to respond to spikes in the incoming load.
elements.sent-2411-223: In Section~\ref{sec:eval , we evaluate a HotMem-aware FaaS runtime based on Openwhisk.. Scaling Up.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Figure~\ref{fig:runtime (right) shows the steps that take place when the runtime decides to spawn a new function instance to scale up resources for function A.}
elements.sent-2411-225: The runtime sends a request to to the agent that runs inside the VM worker to scale up function instances (1).
elements.sent-2411-226: It also employs the hypervisor in the host to send a plug request to the guest, asking to add memory equal to the requirements of the instance, i.e., the memory size pre-defined by the user (2).
elements.sent-2411-227: The HotMem plug path is triggered, and the guest populates a HotMem partition with the added memory (3).
elements.sent-2411-228: The agent then spawns a new instance using the HotMem syscall interface (4).
elements.sent-2411-229: A new container is created and starts executing with all its private allocations being served by and isolated in the just-plugged HotMem partition.. Scaling Down.
elements.sent-2411-230: Figure~\ref{fig:runtime (left) shows the steps that take place when the runtime decides to evict a function instance to scale down resources for function B.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The agent that runs inside the VM shuts down the function container and informs the runtime for the successful down-scaling (5).
elements.sent-2411-232: The runtime then employs the hypervisor in the host to send an unplug request to the guest asking to reclaim memory equal to the memory freed by the recycle (6).
elements.sent-2411-233: The HotMem fast unplug path is triggered in the guest, which i) identifies the empty HotMem zone of the recycled container, ii) directly removes it without any migrations, and iii) notifies the hypervisor (7).. VM creation.
elements.sent-2411-234: Another event that must be adjusted when a serverless runtime adopts the HotMem interface is VM creation.
elements.sent-2411-235: As discussed earlier when the runtime creates a VM that will host HotMem function instances it must declare during boot i) the HotMem private partition size, matching the memory resource limit set for the function, ii) the shared HotMem partition size, matching the size of the runtime and language dependencies of the function, and iii) the maximum number of function instances that will be deployed in the VM (concurrency factor $N$).
elements.sent-2411-236: We evaluate HotMem on a Intel server with two NUMA Nodes.
elements.sent-2411-237: Each node has 10 cores and 128GiB of memory each.
elements.sent-2411-238: We disable SMT in order to reduce variance in the results.
elements.sent-2411-239: The dynamically adjusted VMs, running the workloads, are configured with 10 cores and pinned to the same node (CPUs and memory).
elements.sent-2411-240: The initial VM memory size varies depending on the maximum hotplug memory, so that it can accommodate the required metadata (page structs) for the maximum hotplugged size ($inital\_memory = max\_memory * page\_struct\_size / page\_size$).
elements.sent-2411-241: The maximum hotplug memory is always tied to the workload requirements and maximum concurrency and remains the same among experiments that compare the different mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-242: We choose the Cloud Hypervisor (v38.0) as the VMM which includes a well tested rust implementation of the virtio-mem~\cite{virtio-mem hotplug mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-243: We build HotMem on Linux Kernel 6.6.30.

elements.sent-2411-244: Table~\ref{tab:functions summarizes the serverless workloads used for evaluating HotMem.}
elements.sent-2411-245: We use the image classification benchmark (Cnn) from FunctionBench~\cite{functionbench and three representative real-world serverless workloads, also used in previous works~\cite{faasmem : the BERT-based ML inference~\cite{bert (Bert), a graph breadth-first-search workload (BFS) and an HTML web server (HTML).}}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Based on previous works~\cite{faasmem , we first experiment with the function resource limits on our setup until we achieve an acceptable response latency based on typical FaaS SLOs.}
elements.sent-2411-247: We then assign different vCPU and memory limits to each workload, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:functions .}
elements.sent-2411-248: Unplug latency: In all our experiments, the duration of the unplug request is obtained in the hypervisor and represents the time elapsed for an unplug request received by the hypervisor until the latter marks the reclaimed VM memory as MADV\_DONT-NEED, in order to immediately release the requested size to the host.
elements.sent-2411-249: We obtain this metric from the Cloud Hypervisor tracing framework by slightly changing a few lines of code.
elements.sent-2411-250: Function Latency We report the tail latency (P99) of serverless functions, to showcase the effect of vanilla and HotMem (un)plugging on function execution.
elements.sent-2411-251: We also report the per-second average of function end-to-end latency, in order to more accurately highlight the unplugging mechanism impact on function performance during shrinking events.. CPU Usage.
elements.sent-2411-252: We measure the CPU usage of the unplugging mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-253: We first enforce the guest kernel driver to always run on a specific vCPU, isolated from the micro benchmark process, by setting the virtio-mem interrupt affinity inside the guest.
elements.sent-2411-254: The corresponding vCPU thread is pinned to a specific physical core and additionally placed inside a threaded cgroup on the host.
elements.sent-2411-255: The same applies for the corresponding VMM virtio-mem thread on the host.
elements.sent-2411-256: We report the vCPU usage of the unplugging mechanism as measured by the CPU Accounting cgroup controller.
elements.sent-2411-257: We first evaluate the HotMem mechanism unplug speed and CPU usage using the memhog microbenchmark~\cite{memhog .}
elements.sent-2411-258: Memhog repeatedly allocates and deallocates a specified amount of memory and as a side-effect it also stresses the CPU and memory of the host.
elements.sent-2411-259: We spawn multiple memhog processes on HotMem partitions (HotMem) and on ZONE\_MOVABLE (vanilla) and after a while we demand some of the memory back.
elements.sent-2411-260: The reclaim step involves freeing the memory by stopping memhog and immediately ask the hypervisor to unplug.
elements.sent-2411-261: We choose sizes that are representative of typical serverless memory limits in order to evaluate the unplugging speed of different HotMem partitions.. Serverless functions.
elements.sent-2411-262: We evaluate 4 functions from FunctionBench~\cite{functionbench and ~\cite{faasmem , presented in Table 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: We deploy every function inside a dedicated VM, following the multi-container-per-VM model.
elements.sent-2411-264: We use Azure traces with bursty request patterns~\cite{azuretrace to drive the invocations of every function and rely on a FaaS runtime to scale up and down function instances based on the incoming load (described next).}
elements.sent-2411-265: We study the performance of three different configurations: a) using HotMem, using HotMem-aware virtio-mem, to scale up and down the VM resources along with the instances, b) using Vanilla with vanilla virtio-mem for the same purpose, and c) running the functions inside statically over-provisioned VMs (fixed maximum memory size throughout their lifecycle).
elements.sent-2411-266: We report the throughput (MiB/sec) with which HotMem (a) and Vanilla (b) reclaims memory for every function.
elements.sent-2411-267: We additionally report the tail latency of successful function invocations for all configurations.
elements.sent-2411-268: Finally we study the impact of VM memory shrinking events (unplug) on the performance of other instances concurrently running inside the guest.
elements.sent-2411-269: We build upon an Openwhisk-based runtime~\cite{faasmem , by adding the required functionality to spawn secure containers inside VMs and resize the VM memory on demand, based on the number of concurrent existing function instances.}
elements.sent-2411-270: The serverless runtime is responsible of issuing incoming request to the VM.
elements.sent-2411-271: A dispatcher process (Agent) inside the VM handles the incoming requests as described in Section~\ref{sec:runtime and shown in Figure~\ref{fig:runtime .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: The Agent maintains a pool of idle containers per function.
elements.sent-2411-273: When no idle container is available for an incoming request, Agent scales up the the number of function instances.
elements.sent-2411-274: After a function instance successfully handles an incoming request, its container is placed back to the idle pool.
elements.sent-2411-275: A recycling operation, on a regular basis, asks the Agent to scale down the number of instances corresponding to any containers that are idle for more than the maximum keep-alive time.
elements.sent-2411-276: The Agent shrinks the VM memory based on the number of destroyed containers.
elements.sent-2411-277: We evaluate the function memory reclamation throughput based on the shrinking events through the experiment lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-278: In order to get more accurate results the Agent equally pins the function instances to vCPUs based on the pre-configured vCPU weight per workload as shown in Table 1.
elements.sent-2411-279: For experimenting reasons we also add the option to choose specific vCPUs per request invocation.
elements.sent-2411-280: We spawn multiple instances of the memhog program as described in Section \ref{sec:meth:eval , in order to allocate almost all the free memory inside the VM and then release different amounts to be reclaimed by the hypervisor.}
elements.sent-2411-281: We experiment with different plugged sizes and reclaim request sizes.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/Results/ubench_latency} \caption{Average latency (ms) to reclaim memory of different sizes from a guest with memhog-loaded CPUs.} \label{fig:ubench:latency}
elements.sent-2411-282: We first measure the raw reclamation speed for HotMem and Vanilla.
elements.sent-2411-283: Figure \ref{fig:ubench:latency shows the reclamation latency for different memory sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-284: HotMem reclamation is an order of magnitude faster than Vanilla as it totally avoids busy pages migration.
elements.sent-2411-285: We also note that as the requested size increases, the latency increases accordingly in both cases.
elements.sent-2411-286: This is related to the fact that Linux (un)plugs memory in the granularity of memory blocks (128MiB), as explained in Section~\ref{sec:background:linux .}
elements.sent-2411-287: The virtio-mem driver releases the memory in the same granularity.
elements.sent-2411-288: As a result, as the requested memory size increases, the operations invoked are multiplied in respect to the number of released memory blocks.
elements.sent-2411-289: We consider optimizing virtio-mem and Linux to handle unplug requests at larger granularities as future work.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/Results/percentage} \caption{Reclaiming 2GiB out of 64GiB of hotplugged memory as the overall memory usage increases. Linux achieves better reclamation speed when the VM has more free memory as less page migrations occur. HotMem performs extremely faster as it decouples the reclamation speed from the availability of free pages.} \label{fig:2gPercentUsage}
elements.sent-2411-290: The performance of Vanilla is significantly affected by the number of free pages available inside the guest.
elements.sent-2411-291: We experiment on a VM with 64GB of plugged memory and repeatedly request to reclaim 2GiB out of 64GiB, while the guest memory usage increases.
elements.sent-2411-294: HotMem has predictable and stable performance which is decoupled from the guest load.
elements.sent-2411-295: This stems from completely eliminating page migrations from the unplug path by making the hotplug driver aware of free (empty) HotMem partitions.
elements.sent-2411-296: Vanilla latency exhibits an upward trend along with the memory usage, as the number of potentially busy pages per memory block increases, resulting in expensive migrations during unplug operations.
elements.sent-2411-297: Vanilla performance is unpredictable, i.e., there are fluctuations in performance, as the memhog benchmark inside the guest allocates pages randomly while vanilla virtio-mem tries to unplug memory blocks~\cite{virtio-mem .}
elements.sent-2411-298: HotMem instead instructs the virtio-mem driver to quickly aim for the unused memory blocks of an empty HotMem partition providing stable performance.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figures/Results/cpuusage-vcpu} \caption{Cumulative CPU utilization of the vCPU thread that serves the unplug requests for a VM with 16GiB hotplugged memory, shrinking back to 512MiB in 32 steps of 512MiB. HotMem only slightly uses the vCPU during reclamation. Vanilla keeps the core busy by migrating pages. We also note that this is the reason that the Vanilla experiment lasts longer.} \label{fig:cpuusage:vcpu}
elements.sent-2411-299: We also study the CPU usage of the two mechanisms, following the methodology in Section \ref{sec:meth .}
elements.sent-2411-300: Figure \ref{fig:cpuusage:vcpu shows the cumulative CPU usage measured when we resize a VM with initially 16GiB of memory down to 512MiB in 32 reclamation steps, i.e., 32 unplug operations of 512MB each.}
elements.sent-2411-301: We notice that HotMem only slightly uses the vCPU throughout the experiment, while the vanilla mechanism keeps the CPU occupied in every step.
elements.sent-2411-302: Vanilla's CPU utilization is attributed to the page migrations that take place during unplug operations and as we show later can penalize performance of other processes concurrently running inside the VM.
elements.sent-2411-303: The step-wise increase represents the idle periods between the reclamation steps.
elements.sent-2411-304: The line for HotMem is shorter than vanilla, as it finishes the unplugging much faster (Section~\ref{sec:eval:ubench:uspeed ).}
elements.sent-2411-305: We select 4 Azure traces, map them randomly to our functions and use them to drive their load.
elements.sent-2411-306: Each function is placed in a separate VM, following the multiple-container-single-VM model.
elements.sent-2411-307: The maximum concurrency for each function is configured based on the VM vCPU count (10) divided by the Assigned vCPUs according to Table~\ref{tab:functions .}
elements.sent-2411-308: It is passed as a boot parameter to its corresponding HotMem VMs along with the function's memory requirements (Section~\ref{sec:implementation ).}
elements.sent-2411-309: HotMem uses this information to build its partitions.
elements.sent-2411-310: For this experiment, the FaaS runtime (Section~\ref{sec:meth ) dynamically scales up and down the number of instances per function, within the corresponding VMs, to respond to the incoming load.}
elements.sent-2411-311: The scale up is combined with plug operations to size up the VM memory to allow hosting the new instances.
elements.sent-2411-312: Symmetrically, the scale down is combined with unplug operations that size down the VM to release the memory that was freed by recycling instances.
elements.sent-2411-313: At the beginning of the traces used, there are bursts of requests that trigger the creation of multiple function instances (cold starts) and thus multiple plug events.
elements.sent-2411-314: Later the load drops abruptly, and the runtime starts to evict idle function instances.
elements.sent-2411-315: Figure~\ref{fig:faas:throughput reports the throughput that each interface manages to reclaim memory during down-scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-316: We observe that HotMem achieves 7x higher throughput.
elements.sent-2411-317: Figure~\ref{fig:faas:tail also reports the tail latency (99th percentile) of the requests per function for the same trace and for all interfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-318: We also report the tail latency measured when we run the same experiment but using over-provisioned VMs, i.e., configured to have the maximum memory required throughout the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-319: These results study the impact of plug latency, which can penalize cold invocations.
elements.sent-2411-320: First we observe that vanilla and HotMem have comparable performance, as expected, since HotMem builds on top of the already optimized virtio-mem interface.
elements.sent-2411-321: More interestingly, we observe that dynamically hot(un)plugging memory while scaling up and down instances, using any interface, has comparable performance to using statically over-provisioned VMs.
elements.sent-2411-322: The plug operations do not increase the tail latency.
elements.sent-2411-323: Only Bert performance is slightly affected, for which we measure that plug operations take $\approx$30ms.
elements.sent-2411-324: These results show that elasticity does not necessarily penalize performance.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figures/Results/faas_throughput} \caption{Throughput (MiB/sec) of reclaiming memory while scaling up and down instances based on a realistic FaaS trace.} \label{fig:faas:throughput}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/Results/interference} \caption{Average per second end-to-end latency for invocations of the Cnn function, when its instances are co-located with instances of the HTML function, for a time period of 300 seconds while we drive the system with requests from a real Azure trace. Around the 125 and 220 seconds mark we notice two peaks in the vanilla case which last for a few seconds each. At these timestamps the FaaS runtime shrinks the VM memory after scaling down the HTML instances due to a drop in its incoming load. HotMem, instead, does not interfere with CNN execution as on the same events simply removes empty partitions avoiding any migrations.} \label{fig:interference}
elements.sent-2411-325: To investigate the impact of unplugging events on instances running on the same VM, we randomly choose two traces from the Azure Trace ~\cite{azuretrace as inputs for two of our workloads (Cnn and HTML) with similar memory requirements (same HotMem partition size: 384MB) and co-locate them on the same VM.}
elements.sent-2411-326: We instruct the runtime to pin the Cnn instances on 2 specific cores, one of which also serves the virtio-mem interrupts inside the guest.
elements.sent-2411-327: The other workload is pinned to the other 8 vCPUs.
elements.sent-2411-328: The maximum number of instances for each function are adjusted according to the trace load and the vCPU assigned from Table \ref{tab:functions (Cnn 4, HTML 40).}
elements.sent-2411-329: The runtime spawns the maximum amount of containers in order to handle the load.
elements.sent-2411-330: The idle lifetime is configured to 120 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-331: Figure~\ref{fig:interference shows the average end-to-end latency per second for the Cnn requests.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Vanilla shows a significant spike at around $\approx$125s of the experiment when the latency shows an increase of more than 100\%.
elements.sent-2411-333: This is when the first shrinking event happens, after the runtime decides to evict 17 of the total spawned HTML function instances that were spawned more than 120 seconds ago in order to serve the initial burst of incoming requests.
elements.sent-2411-334: During this period the unplugging driver inside the guest has been scheduled to migrate pages using one of the vCPUs that are also assigned to the Cnn instances.
elements.sent-2411-335: The CPU utilization caused by the migrations (Figure~\ref{fig:cpuusage:vcpu ) penalizes the performance of Cnn function instances.}
elements.sent-2411-336: A similar event occurs around the 225s of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-337: On the other hand, the Cnn instances on the HotMem VM configuration experience no similar impact during the scale down of HTML instances due to the zero-migrations design of HotMem.
elements.sent-2411-338: We omit the HTML results as they exhibit the same performance for both configurations.
elements.sent-2411-339: The HTML function instances are pinned to a different subset of vCPUs, which don't include the vCPU that handles the resize requests.
elements.sent-2411-340: As a result, they experience no CPU interference during shrinking events.
elements.sent-2411-341: We conclude that vanilla hot(un)plugging causes high CPU utilization during shrinking events due to page migration and we show how the workloads running inside the VM are affected by it.
elements.sent-2411-342: HotMem overcomes this interference by eliminating the CPU cycles needed for migrations.
elements.sent-2411-343: HotMem supports fast and efficient VM shrinking for serverless workloads and overcomes the need for redirecting the load to other VM workers during unplugging events, as happens in other related works (Section \ref{sec:related ).}
elements.sent-2411-344: To realize memory elasticity on physical machines, Infiniswap~\cite{infiniswap relies on memory disaggregation implemented via a swapping-based interface over RDMA fabrics.}
elements.sent-2411-345: FluidMem~\cite{fluidmem targets VMs instead and improves upon the swap-based approach by utilizing the Linux userfaultfd interface~\cite{userfaultfd .}}
elements.sent-2411-346: Both Infiniswap and Fluidmem target disaggregated RDMA-accessible memory.
elements.sent-2411-347: By contrast, HotMem targets FaaS microVMs and, by providing reliable and fast memory reclamation, enables the coupling of memory hot(un)plugging with function invocation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Memory ballooning~\cite{hotplugorballon,resizingballoon,balloonvmware,appballoon,meb,hubballon has been the state-of-practice interface to implement VM memory elasticity.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Memory ballooning implements a paravirtualized interface, using a guest balloon driver controlled by the hypervisor.
elements.sent-2411-350: The VM is booted with a target maximum memory size and the hypervisor triggers the driver to inflate or deflate the balloon and decrease or increase the VM's available memory accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-351: When the hypervisor needs to reclaim memory, the guest driver allocates guest physical pages and reports reclaimed memory to the hypervisor, which can then be reused.
elements.sent-2411-352: To increase guest memory, the driver releases previously reclaimed memory back to the guest OS, increasing the VM's available memory.
elements.sent-2411-353: Ballooning is known to suffer from long inflation latencies as it triggers guest OS allocations that delay reclamation, especially when guest memory is fragmented~\cite{resizingballoon, analysis, virtio-mem .}
elements.sent-2411-354: A recent work~\cite{mhvm that uses ballooning to harvest idle VM memory resources, employs a large set of optimizations to work around the method's high latency, e.g., reserving idle memory buffers to fall back to use when inflation is late to secure free blocks of memory.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Swap-based approaches~\cite{vswapper,memflex have also been used to improve VM memory elasticity using local (hypervisor) resources (memory), usually synergistically with memory ballooning.}
elements.sent-2411-356: Previously, transcendent memory~\cite{tmem,tmem2 also used a swap-based interface to enable seamless VM memory scaling, via the Frontswap mechanism~\cite{frontswap .}}
elements.sent-2411-357: In order to meet the extreme scalability and elasticity demands of the FaaS serverless computing paradigm, various techniques have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-358: One line of research focuses on accelerating container sandboxing for serverless functions~\cite{MXFaas,sock,sand,icebreaker .}
elements.sent-2411-359: By contrast, we focus on microVM-based sandboxing.
elements.sent-2411-360: Snowflock~\cite{snowflock proposes VM cloning for scaling cloud workloads.}
elements.sent-2411-361: We instead target FaaS functions running on microVMs.
elements.sent-2411-362: Recently, microVM snapshotting has been proposed~\cite{catalyzer,vhive,faasnap,iosnaps in order to accelerate function cold-starts.}
elements.sent-2411-363: HotMem unlocks increased performance by using lean microVMs, which can rapidly scale to accommodate multiple concurrent function invocations, thus avoiding the microVM initialization tax, and enabling functions to share the warmed-up state of other concurrently running functions on the same microVM.
elements.sent-2411-364: Harvesting of unused resources has been studied both in the context of virtual machines~\cite{harvest,hvm,smartharvest,snape and serverless computing~\cite{harvestserverless .}}
elements.sent-2411-365: While these works focus on harvesting idle compute resources, i.e., CPUs, others~\cite{mhvm,libra,ofc,deflation,vmdeflation also support harvesting idle memory resources.}
elements.sent-2411-366: More specifically, ~\cite{mhvm evaluates harvesting memory with serverless workloads by introducing a number of optimizations including the preservation of large buffers of idle memory, proactive reclamation, and larger reclamation chunks.}
elements.sent-2411-367: These optimizations aim to minimize the shrinking event impact on allocating resources for higher priority workloads but are not minimizing page migrations during unplug.
elements.sent-2411-368: The incoming requests to function instances are redirected to other VM workers during shrinking events in order to avoid the performance penalty.
elements.sent-2411-369: HotMem builds upon the concept of idle memory harvesting in the context of FaaS and eliminates the shrinking event impact by targeting free memory ranges to avoid the migration overhead.
elements.sent-2411-370: By providing fast and reliable VM memory reclamation, HotMem minimizes memory stranding and enables the rapid reuse of released memory for subsequent function invocations.
elements.sent-2411-371: We present HotMem, a novel memory management framework to enable the rapid reclamation of hotplugged memory in virtual machines, tailored for VM-sandboxed serverless functions.
elements.sent-2411-372: HotMem multiplexes functions on lean microVMs and hotplugs function memory on a per-function basis.
elements.sent-2411-373: By segregating hotplugged memory from normal VM memory, HotMem bounds the lifetimes of allocations on hotplugged memory to the lifetime of the function associated function, ensuring that hotplugged memory is readily reclaimable on function exit.
elements.sent-2411-374: HotMem is able to reclaim memory of terminated serverless functions an order of magnitude faster than state-of-the-art and achieves comparable tail latency with a setup that relies on statically over-provisioned VMs.
elements.sent-2411-375: Finally, HotMem eliminates the performance impact on co-located function instances during VM shrinking, as it totally obviates the need for page migrations on the reclaim path.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
